section.title: Ministers of the New Covenant
section.sectionRationale: Paul answers the charge that he needs letters of recommendation by pointing to the Corinthians themselves as a living letter written by the Spirit on human hearts. This launches the letter's great meditation on the two covenants: the ministry of the letter engraved on stone, which came with a fading glory, and the ministry of the Spirit, which comes with a surpassing and enduring glory. The veil that lay over Moses' face and still lies over unbelieving hearts is removed only in Christ, so that with unveiled faces believers behold the Lord's glory and are transformed into His image.
section.thesis: God has qualified His servants as ministers of a new covenant, not of the letter that kills but of the Spirit who gives life; the fading glory of Moses is eclipsed by the surpassing and enduring glory now revealed in Christ, and all who turn to the Lord have the veil removed and are transformed from glory to glory into His image.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the Corinthians as a letter from Christ written by the Spirit; the surpassing glory of the ministry of the Spirit over the fading glory of the letter; and the veil removed in Christ so that with unveiled faces we are transformed into His image.

units.label: A letter from Christ
units.note: written by the Spirit on human hearts

verses.text: Are we beginning to commend ourselves again? Or do we need, like some people, letters of recommendation to you or from you?
You yourselves are our letter, inscribed on our hearts, known and read by everyone.

It is clear that you are a letter from Christ, the result of our ministry, written not with ink but with the {{pneuma|Spirit}} of the living God, not on tablets of stone but on tablets of human hearts.
Such confidence before God is ours through Christ.
Not that we are competent in ourselves to claim that anything comes from us, but our competence comes from God.
And He has qualified us as ministers of a new {{diatheke|covenant}}, not of the {{gramma|letter}} but of the Spirit; for the letter kills, but the Spirit gives life.

units.label: The surpassing glory of the new covenant
units.note: the fading glory of the letter and the enduring glory of the Spirit

verses.text: Now if the ministry of death, which was engraved in letters on stone, came with such {{doxa|glory}} that the Israelites could not gaze at the face of Moses because of its fleeting glory,
will not the ministry of the Spirit be even more glorious?
For if the ministry of condemnation was glorious, how much more glorious is the ministry of righteousness!
Indeed, what was once glorious has no glory now in comparison to the glory that surpasses it.
For if what was fading away came with glory, how much greater is the glory of that which endures!

units.label: The veil removed in Christ
units.note: unveiled faces, transformed from glory to glory

verses.text: Therefore, since we have such a hope, we are very bold.
We are not like Moses, who would put a veil over his face to keep the Israelites from gazing at the end of what was fading away.
But their minds were closed. For to this day the same veil remains at the reading of the old covenant. It has not been lifted, because only in Christ can it be removed.
And even to this day when Moses is read, a veil covers their hearts.
But whenever anyone turns to the Lord, the veil is taken away.
Now the Lord is the Spirit, and where the Spirit of the Lord is, there is freedom.
And we, who with unveiled faces all reflect the glory of the Lord, are being {{metamorphoo|transformed}} into His image with intensifying glory, which comes from the Lord, who is the Spirit.

pneuma.gloss: Spirit, breath, wind. Here it is the Holy Spirit of the living God, who writes the new covenant not with ink on stone but on the living tablets of human hearts. Paul contrasts the Spirit who gives life with the letter that kills: the Spirit is the very power and glory of the new covenant ministry. Where the Spirit of the Lord is, there is freedom, and by the Spirit believers are transformed into the image of Christ.

diatheke.gloss: Covenant, a binding arrangement that God Himself establishes and guarantees. Paul calls his ministry a ministry of a new covenant, the covenant promised in Jeremiah 31 in which God writes His law on the heart and gives His Spirit. Unlike the old covenant engraved on stone, the new covenant is inward, life giving, and enduring. It rests not on human competence but on God, who qualifies His servants and fulfils His promise in Christ.

gramma.gloss: Letter, the written character or code, standing for the law of Moses as an external written command. Paul says the letter kills: the law engraved on stone reveals sin and pronounces condemnation and death, but it cannot give the life or the power to obey that it demands. It is not that the law is evil, for it came with real glory; rather it belongs to a ministry that condemns, now surpassed by the Spirit who makes alive.

doxa.gloss: Glory, radiance, weighty splendour. The old covenant truly came with glory, seen in the shining of Moses' face, yet that glory was fading and had to be veiled. The glory of the new covenant surpasses it and endures, for it is the glory of the Lord Himself beheld with unveiled faces. This same glory grows in believers as they are changed into Christ's image from one degree of glory to the next.

metamorphoo.gloss: To transform, to change form, the word from which we get metamorphosis. It is used of Christ's transfiguration and of the renewing of the believer's mind in Romans 12:2. Here it describes the ongoing, Spirit wrought change of those who behold the Lord's glory with unveiled faces, so that they are progressively remade into His image with intensifying glory. It is passive: we are being transformed by the Lord, who is the Spirit, not by our own effort.

basic.context: Paul's opponents apparently carried letters of recommendation and questioned his credentials. Paul answers that he needs no such letter, for the Corinthian church itself, changed by the gospel, is his letter of recommendation, written by the Spirit of God on human hearts. This opens a rich comparison between the old covenant given through Moses and the new covenant of the Spirit, and shows how the glory that once shone on Moses' face is far surpassed by the glory now revealed in Christ.
basic.hermeneutics: Paul is reading the account of Moses' shining and veiled face in Exodus 34 and drawing out its deeper meaning. He is not disparaging the law as evil; he calls it glorious. His point is comparative: a ministry that condemns is glorious, but a ministry that gives righteousness and the Spirit is far more glorious, and it endures where the old was fading. Read the whole passage as a contrast of two ministries, not two Gods or two Bibles.
basic.originalLanguages: The new covenant is diatheke, the arrangement God promised in Jeremiah 31. The contrast is between gramma, the written letter that kills, and pneuma, the Spirit who gives life. The old ministry had doxa, glory, but a fading glory, while the new has surpassing and enduring glory. And by the Spirit believers are transformed, metamorphoo, into Christ's image.
basic.typology: Moses with the veil over his shining face is the type; Christ and His unveiled people are the fulfilment. The old covenant engraved on tablets of stone foreshadows the new covenant written by the Spirit on hearts of flesh, as Jeremiah and Ezekiel promised. The fading glory of Sinai points forward to the abiding glory of the risen Lord beheld by faith.
basic.christ: Christ is the one in whom the veil is removed, the Lord to whom the heart turns and finds the covering taken away. He is the image of God into which believers are being transformed, and His glory is the glory now beheld with unveiled faces. The whole old covenant, rightly read, points to Him, and only in Him is it truly understood.
basic.god: God is the one who makes His servants competent, so that the ministry rests on His power and not on human ability. He is the living God who writes on human hearts by His Spirit, the God who keeps His covenant promise, and the God whose glory shines in the face of Christ. The Lord, the Spirit, brings freedom and transforms His people.
basic.crossReferences: Jeremiah 31 promises the new covenant written on the heart. Ezekiel 36 promises a new heart and God's Spirit within. Exodus 34 records the shining and veiling of Moses' face. Romans 8 says the Spirit gives life where the law could not.
basic.oneStory: The Bible moves from a covenant written on stone that could command but not empower, to a new covenant in which God writes His law on the heart by His Spirit. Paul stands as a minister of that new covenant, and its glory, the glory of Christ, so surpasses the old that the fading radiance of Sinai is eclipsed. All who turn to the Lord have the veil lifted and are changed into His image.

context.literary: This chapter continues the defense of Paul's ministry begun in chapter two and flows directly into chapter four, where he speaks of the treasure in jars of clay. Verses 1 to 3 answer the question of recommendation letters; verses 4 to 6 ground his confidence in God; verses 7 to 11 argue from lesser to greater about the two ministries; and verses 12 to 18 apply the image of Moses' veil. The argument builds to the soaring climax of verse 18.
context.historical: Letters of recommendation were common in the ancient world to vouch for a traveller's standing. Paul's rivals seem to have used them to undercut him. Behind the whole passage lies the memory of Sinai, where Moses' face shone after meeting with God and he veiled it before the people, an event every Jewish reader knew from Exodus 34.
context.canonical: Paul here weaves together the new covenant promises of Jeremiah and Ezekiel with the narrative of Exodus 34, showing the unity of Scripture. The chapter has been foundational for Christian understanding of the relationship between law and Spirit, old covenant and new, and for the doctrine of progressive sanctification in verse 18.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To vindicate the legitimacy and surpassing glory of Paul's new covenant ministry against critics who trusted in credentials and outward appearance, and to show that the law, glorious as it was, has been eclipsed by the life giving ministry of the Spirit in Christ, into whose image believers are now being transformed.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The comparison of the two covenants is doctrinal teaching that abides. The narrative of Moses' veil is used illustratively, not as a rule to imitate. The promise that believers are transformed from glory to glory is a description of what the Spirit does, and by implication an invitation to keep beholding the Lord.

debates.question: In what sense does the letter kill?

views.label: The law reveals sin and pronounces condemnation
views.case: The written law exposes transgression and sentences the sinner to death, since it demands a righteousness it cannot supply; Romans 7 develops the same point, that the commandment meant for life brought death.

views.label: A merely external, literal religion apart from the Spirit
views.case: Some read letter and Spirit as two ways of relating to God: an outward, literalistic observance that deadens, versus inward life in the Spirit; this captures a real danger but risks blunting Paul's covenantal contrast.

debates.bearing: Either way the point stands that no written code can give life; life comes only through the Spirit of the new covenant, so the passage drives us from self effort to Christ.

debates.question: Does with unveiled faces reflect or behold the glory of the Lord?

views.label: Beholding as in a mirror
views.case: Many render it that believers contemplate the Lord's glory, and by that gazing are transformed, which fits the mirror imagery and the theme of turning to the Lord.

views.label: Reflecting the glory
views.case: Others take it that believers, like Moses, reflect the glory they have received, so that the transformation is also displayed outward; the BSB reads reflect.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that the glory is Christ's and the transformation is the Spirit's work; whether by beholding or reflecting, the believer is changed into the image of the Lord.

study.originalLanguages: The governing word is diatheke, covenant: Paul is a minister of the new covenant God promised through Jeremiah, written by the Spirit on the heart. He sets gramma, the letter or written code, against pneuma, the Spirit; the letter kills because it can condemn but not empower, while the Spirit gives life. The old ministry had real doxa, glory, seen in Moses' shining face, but it was fading and had to be veiled, whereas the new covenant's glory surpasses and endures. The climax comes with metamorphoo, transformed: beholding the Lord's glory with unveiled faces, believers are progressively changed by the Spirit into the very image of Christ.

typology.type: The law engraved on tablets of stone
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in the new covenant written by the Spirit on tablets of human hearts, as Jeremiah 31 and Ezekiel 36 promised.

typology.type: The fading glory on Moses' veiled face
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in the enduring glory of the Lord beheld with unveiled faces in Christ, who is the image of God.
Fulfilled and surpassed by the ministry of the Spirit, which brings righteousness and life rather than condemnation and death.

study.christ: Christ is at the centre of the whole comparison. He is the one in whom the veil over the old covenant is removed, the Lord to whom a person turns and finds the covering taken away. He is the image of God, and His glory is the glory believers behold with unveiled faces and into which they are transformed. The Corinthians are a letter from Christ, written by His Spirit. Rightly read, all of Moses points to Him, and only in Him is Scripture unveiled.
study.god: God is the source of all sufficiency for ministry, making weak servants competent so that the power is His. He is the living God who writes on human hearts by His Spirit, the covenant keeping God who fulfils His promise of a new covenant, and the God whose glory shines in the face of Christ. As the Lord, the Spirit, He brings freedom and transforms His people from one degree of glory to the next, so that the whole work of salvation is His gift and His glory.

crossReferences.note: The new covenant in which God writes His law on the heart and forgives sin, the covenant Paul now ministers.
God promises a new heart and to put His Spirit within His people so they walk in His ways.
Moses' face shines after meeting God, and he veils it before the people, the narrative Paul interprets.
What the law could not do, God did in Christ, and the Spirit gives life where the letter condemned.

study.oneStory: Scripture moves from a covenant carved on stone, which could command righteousness but never produce it, to a new covenant in which God Himself writes on the heart by His Spirit. Paul is a minister of that new covenant, and its glory is the glory of Christ, so surpassing that the once dazzling radiance of Sinai fades before it. Everyone who turns to the Lord has the veil removed and, beholding His glory, is remade into His image, until the transformation is complete in the glory to come.

quiz.q: What did Paul mean by calling the Corinthians his letter of recommendation?

options.text: Their changed lives, written by the Spirit on human hearts, proved the truth of his ministry.
options.note: Verses 2 to 3 make the church itself the living evidence, written by the Spirit.

options.text: They had physically written him a glowing reference to carry.
options.note: Paul means a living letter of transformed lives, not an actual written reference.

options.text: They had recommended him to other churches by mail.
options.note: The point is what the Spirit wrote in them, not correspondence they sent.

options.text: He needed their approval before he could preach.
options.note: Paul's confidence comes from God, not from human approval.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean when he says the letter kills but the Spirit gives life?

options.text: The written law reveals sin and condemns, but the Spirit of the new covenant gives life.
options.note: Verse 6 contrasts the condemning law with the life giving Spirit.

options.text: The Old Testament is bad and only the New Testament is good.
options.note: Paul calls the old ministry glorious; he contrasts covenants, not good and evil books.

options.text: We should ignore the commandments entirely.
options.note: The law is holy; the point is that it cannot give the life it demands.
Paul is not warning against Scripture but explaining law and Spirit.

quiz.q: How does the glory of the new covenant compare to the old?

options.text: It surpasses and outlasts it; the old glory was real but fading, the new endures.
options.note: Verses 7 to 11 argue from lesser to greater that the new glory surpasses and endures.

options.text: The old covenant had no glory at all.
options.note: Paul insists the old ministry did come with glory, only a fading one.

options.text: They are exactly equal in glory.
options.note: Paul stresses how much greater the new covenant glory is.

options.text: The new covenant has less glory than the old.
options.note: The whole argument is that the new glory is far greater.

quiz.q: According to verse 18, how are believers transformed into the image of Christ?

options.text: By the Spirit, as with unveiled faces they behold the glory of the Lord.
options.note: The transformation is the Spirit's work as we behold the Lord's glory.

options.text: By trying harder to imitate Jesus in their own strength.
options.note: The verb is passive; the Lord, the Spirit, transforms us as we behold Him.

options.text: All at once, the moment they believe, with nothing ongoing.
options.note: It is a progressive change, from one degree of glory to another.

options.text: By keeping the law engraved on stone.
options.note: The law could not give life; transformation comes by the Spirit in Christ.

questions: Paul pointed to changed lives as proof of real ministry. What in your life would show that the Spirit has been writing on your heart?
The law can tell us what is right but cannot give us power to do it. Where are you relying on rules or self effort instead of the life giving Spirit?
Paul says the glory of the old covenant, real as it was, faded before the greater glory of Christ. What lesser glories are you tempted to cling to that keep you from Christ's greater glory?
The veil is removed when a person turns to the Lord. Is there any area where your heart is still veiled, where you need to turn afresh to Christ?
We are changed into Christ's image by beholding His glory. What practical habits help you behold the Lord, and how might you give more time to gazing on Him?

translationNotes.note: The letter kills but the Spirit gives life contrasts gramma, the written code of the old covenant, with the pneuma, the Holy Spirit of the new; it is a contrast of covenants and ministries, not a claim that the law is evil.

Now the Lord is the Spirit identifies the Lord of the Exodus narrative with the Spirit at work in the new covenant; it affirms the Spirit's deity and His life giving, freeing work, without collapsing the distinction of the divine persons.
The verb rendered reflect can also be rendered behold as in a mirror; both readings agree the glory is the Lord's and the transformation, metamorphoo, is the Spirit's work in the believer.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 Corinthians 3:1-18. No em dashes were present in this passage; none introduced.
